segments.text: Procurement rules to introduce a new New Zealand impact test that means that when
government agencies are going out to tender and comparing different
proposals, the impact that a tender would have on the
New Zealand economy in terms of job creation, training opportunities,
value added to this economy, is now required to be
weighted as part of that agency's consideration.
Which is to say, don't just look at what's the lowest cost, look at
what's the highest value. And when you're considering value, consider New
Zealand economic impact. And I've been upfront, I think
that'll give a leg up to Kiwi businesses when they're seeking the revenue that can
come from the government contracts.
When did that come in, sorry?
Hold on. Questions to the chair.
Yeah.
You have a supplementary? Yeah.
Yeah, supplementary on that. And thank you for that, Minister.
I was going to ask you a sup on that, which I'll carry on.
So on the procurement processes-
Mm
... the rules have changed in terms of like you just explained.
Is it any easier for Kiwi businesses
to get those contracts or to
actually navigate the processes in terms of just how easy it is to
find your way on
system or anything like that?
Yeah, look, at the same time-
Because we don't-
... as we made those changes, we also looked at
simplifying the rules. So we reduced the number of the rules, because the
way that it had previously operated, you basically needed a PhD
to read through them and navigate them.
So wherever possible, we've simplified them.
In terms of the GET system, the thing that I was really conscious of was that
when I had, as minister, asked questions about, well, what proportion of contracts
do we think go to Kiwi firms, how many Kiwi firms do we think are
bidding, we had really quite a lack of data on all of that.
So the GET system has been updated so that we can capture more data going
forward. Not in a position to share that data with members today, but in the
future,
because of those updates to the GET system, we'll be in a better position to do so.
Thank you.
A question from Belinda Wilson.
I just had a sup on that.
Oh, you have a supplementary?
Yeah.
And can I just say this before you ask your sup,
Vini? I think I might know where you're going because the minister has
gone quite wide, so
I'll just see what your question is, so.
Okay. You can see inside my mind pumping over here.
I think I'm able to do that, so let's see. Yeah.
You mentioned the New Zealand impact test,
Minister.
Mm-hmm.
Can you just give me the date when that sort of applied, so when that would start
to kick in and that we could start seeing examples of how that would play out here
in New Zealand?
Yeah. Look, it came into effect on the 1st of December 2025.
And so that's the fifth
edition of the procurement rules, and that's the ones that were updated with the
new economic benefit rule. And so,
already you would expect that government agencies, whenever they're going out
for tenders, that they are applying those rules, and therefore applying the New
Zealand Economic Impact Test. And I expect in
the coming months to get reporting on how
that is impacting reporting decisions.
Initial reporting
shows that 75% of procurements that occurred between December
2025 and February 2026 included New Zealand
economic benefits. That's great. Thank you.
All right. Krishna Dangiri Manaak you have a supplement?
Yes.
Thanks, Madam Chair. Tena koe, Minister.
Will you be reinstating Māori progressive procurement
targets, given that when they were awarded
3,200 contracts, that was worth 930
million to the Māori and New Zealand economy?
No. Instead, our approach has been to say, in terms of the procurement
rules, that looking at how job creation
and career progression opportunities will be created in
a tender is relevant, and therefore how many New Zealand
businesses are being employed in the delivery of a
contract is a relevant consideration.
And of course,
that supports the Māori workforce and the Māori economy because
many of New Zealand's successful and thriving small businesses are
Māori businesses. And we can see
that there is considerable momentum there with the growth that's been occurring in
the Māori economy. And I have very high confidence that
given the opportunity to bid for government contracts or be part of government
procurement, Māori businesses have every opportunity to succeed.
Okay. A new primary, Ruben Edwards.
Yeah. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Minister, for your time this
afternoon. Just wanted to look at,
you talked earlier in one of your answers about projects being dusted off.
Mm-hmm.
I wanted to look at industry closures, so manufacturing where there's dust settling
on manufacturing.
Mm-hmm.
We're seeing this across New Zealand.
Heinz Watties has confirmed 300 jobs.
McCain shutting a processing plant, hundreds of jobs there.
Kaitaia Timber Mills, 200 jobs in the Far North put at risk.
So we're seeing this across the country.
Can you point to a single appropriation in the budget that'll stop the
next factory from closing?
Well,
the member will be
welcome to a view that says that government budgets should have line
items developing individual factories and funding
the development of state-owned factories.
That's a hark back to a version of communism that I don't support,
because I think what you want to see is the private sector choosing to invest in
manufacturing facilities and to develop manufacturing facilities.
I, like the member, and I'm sure like all of the members around this table,
have been really disappointed to see the closure of those New Zealand food
manufacturers with iconic brands, long-standing relationships
with local suppliers,
long-standing employment of people in communities.
And so when they close or downscale,
it is a very significant and sad impact for their
communities and for the New Zealand economy, and I fully acknowledge that.
What I am given confidence by is the growth, the bounce
back that we've seen in manufacturing in the latest GDP
data. In particular, what that shows is that for the
quarter,
manufacturing grew 1.9%, which was above
most forecasts. And that was quite specifically in relation to the
member's question, not just for non-food manufacturing, but quite
specifically for food manufacturing as well.
So that's showing solid growth, and absolutely we want to see that.
And again, what that comes back to in terms of government budgets is
us creating conditions in which manufacturers are able to
succeed. For them, what that means is a good tax environment, an environment
in which they have export opportunities offshore, and our work with the
UAE on trade missions with India is really relevant
for their future prospects. It's also about ensuring that they can rely
on good infrastructure, that we are continuing to invest in the renewable energy
assets that will lead to more affordable electricity in the long run.
It is about things such as our work to progress rates
caps, which ultimately, rates are a cost that face manufacturers just as they
face households. So I'm confident that we are creating the business conditions
which will allow manufacturers to succeed into the future.
Awesome.
Yeah.
So, in the budget, you've set aside
$815,000 just transitions for those people that you've said
you're sad for because of the loss of their jobs.
Is that the sum total of the investment into
that transition work?
Well, I think the member has a very narrow view of how an economy works, because
ultimately what we want to see is that we have an economy where there are
opportunities for existing manufacturers to expand,
for new manufacturers to establish, so that more jobs are created
in that sector, in that industry for all New Zealanders.
What we also want to see is an economy that's dynamic, which is to
say that there are more jobs and opportunities being created in new places.
And so what I'm buoyed by is the fact that overall, with the
economic foundations that our budget puts in place, we are
forecasting 220,000 jobs being created, new
businesses being created, exports expanding,
and businesses continuing to grow.
Because what that tells me is that in the coming months and years, there will be
more opportunities in communities.
And that is not to say that there haven't been significant
setbacks, that there have been business failures. That is the case.
But as we look to the future, we can see stronger foundations from which
opportunities will grow.
Scott, you have a primary finish? Yeah. Scott Willis.
Yes. Thank you. And when you were talking about investment in renewable
electricity generation-
Mm
... or in lowering electricity prices in particular,
government's put forward a 200 million gas security fund, but is
also funding gas transition,
transition away from gas. Do you think there's some inconsistency, given that we
know that we don't have any more gas-
Mm
... to put money into something that is a dead end, while also
funding that dead end away from that?
Well, respectfully-
Is this a sensible use of public money?
Yes, we-- yes, it is. And respectfully, I don't think it's fair to say that
gas is a dead end in New Zealand. As much as Jacinda Ardern
and James Shaw and Holly Walker tried to make it a dead end, the
reality is that the choice to ban oil and gas
exploration provided a massive Confidence not to those
seeking to explore for natural gas, but it is the case
that many experts will tell you that there are likely more
significant gas reserves in New Zealand.
The challenge has been twofold. One, the ban has put off people
exploring for those gas reserves and investing and extracting them
because they're worried that a future leftist government would
again make a crazy decision to ban that extraction.
Two, natural gas, which has, in the past, provided a
nationally resilient sovereign source of energy for New
Zealand, has depleted at a faster rate than was previously
expected. And that has had a very significant impact,
not only on natural gas supplies for manufacturers using it directly,
but also on wholesale energy prices.
So our response to those two problems is twofold.
On the one hand, we are trying to create more confidence and
sovereign confidence in our people investing in
natural gas exploration and extraction in New Zealand, and that's what the $200
million fund is about. At the same time, we're being
realistic about the fact that the rapidly diminishing supplies of
gas in the very short and medium term is such that
providing some support to firms previously reliant on gas
to transition to electricity is a positive step
to take, not only for those firms who will get
lower cost finance for making that transition, but also every
time a firm transitions away from natural gas to electricity, that means there's
more gas available in the market for those who have no choice but to use
gas for their manufacturing processes.
So is the minister
pleased to be able to adopt the Giddy fund redux to ensure
that industry can-
I would never go near Giddy with a barge pole, and please never compare anything
I have done with it
... support the transition away from gas.
No. I would never take the Giddy approach of massive
multimillion-dollar free checks of corporate welfare to big
businesses to pad their pockets. Instead, what we've taken
is a very prudent approach, which says that we can use a Crown
guarantee to create lower cost finance for
firms that wish to invest their own money in productive assets that will lower
their costs over the medium to long term, and that's the approach that the gas
transition loan scheme takes.
Dr. David Wilson, your primary.
Yeah.
Well, thank you, Chair. Well, it'd be for me to talk about corporate welfare.
Just wondering whether or not
the review has been done into the subsidy that's provided to the film industry,
as promised to the committee earlier.
And if so, was there anything to
be taken from it?
Yeah. Look, as members will recall,
we had a look at the overall
film subsidy, and consulted
with the sector, who gave us feedback that using the same envelope of money, we
could get more impact
if we made some small changes to it, and we announced those
changes last year.
They related to allowing more people access to the
5% uplift and the rebate, particularly for those doing
post-development
work, and changes in the thresholds for who could apply to it.
Now, I've got a particular page in here that I'm trying to turn to, but someone
might-
Which one? Page 21.
There has been some good evidence that those changes have had a positive
impact. Here we go.
As at April 2026, 14 productions
had registered under the new criteria, including four international and
10 PDV projects. Those
productions are expected to generate about 580 million in
qualifying New Zealand production expenditure and about 145 million
in rebate payments. And looking back over the past decade, the number
of screen firms has grown by 38%, mainly in post-production and
visual effects, which is emerging as a real specialty for New Zealand, which
again supports high-paid jobs, and really good careers for New
Zealanders.
So supplementary.
Yeah.
I'm guessing from your answer that the main evaluation has been around
economic impact-
Mm
... rather than cost benefit.
Yes, there has also been cost benefit analysis,
which shows that,
essentially, the cost benefit analysis, I don't have the specific numbers here, is
that for the dollars that we invest through the rebate, we
get a multiple of return in the form of
investment into the New Zealand economy, creation of jobs, and impact that
would not otherwise have occurred.
The film sector is particular in that
internationally it is just a heavily, heavily
subsidized sector. So basically, if you want to show up to the game,
you need to have something there. Now, my view is that New Zealand should never be
racing to the bottom, competing, saying, "We'll give you the biggest subsidy."
Actually, our offering should be, "This is the best place in the world to
make films because we have the most stunning natural environment, the most
talented people," whether it's Peter Jackson or Taika Waititi,
"and we also have good facilities. We're a good place to do business.
We're not going to tie you up in crazy red tape.
And yeah, by the way, we also have a rebate.
It's not as generous as some others, but it's sufficient that you should give us a
look."
Dr. Vanessa Baining.
Thank you very much. Minister, there's about $52 million that
are allocated for capital expenditure
in the previous budget, and I think it's the same for this budget.
What are your priorities for that capital expenditure at MB?
Yeah. So do you mean the overall capital expenditure for the Crown or
you were just meaning for MB?
Well, just for MB. Yeah, just for MB.
Yeah. I'm going to actually ask you, Nick, to speak to the capital investments to
MB over the coming year. Obviously, it's always a combination of-
Is that part of the-
Sorry?
I was going to ask, is that part of the write-off for-
No. Well, the new investments will be a combination of asset
upgrades, technology upgrades, and other upgrades.
But Nick, if I can get you to speak to that.
Yeah. No. I think Paul may have the full list, but it'll be
A combination of buildings, equipment fit out for
offices, it'll be fleet. What's interesting in the technology
space is that you're increasingly seeing a shift from large capital investments to
more operating.
Mm.
So over time, you'll see less of that capital allocation going towards technology
projects. Paul, do you have a more-
I think that's correct. And just for clarity, the biometric project was not a
capital.
Okay.
That was an operating model, so it's not included in that capital project.
As Nick says, a lot of the work that we do, for capital is actually
IT. We lease most of our properties, with the exception of the Mangere
Refugee Centre, which we do own, and we're doing property upgrades in that space,
as you'd imagine.
We are
forecasting over the next four or five years, we'll have a steady reduction in
capital expenditure as we move our IT to software as a service and
technology as a service onto the cloud.
So you see a much smaller capital program, but we need to balance that out with
increased opex.
Mm. Okay. All right. New question. David Benson.
Thank you, Madam Chair.
Subsidies. You've talked about the screen production rebate, the gaming
development sector rebate.
Mm.
You've
seen enormous success, you would agree, from that rebate, as far as
the growth of that industry?
Oh, look, I'm really pleased with the growth that's occurred in that industry, 40%
year on year. So that's great to see.
Fantastic. Did you consider lowering the minimum annual spend for thresholds
for companies to be eligible for the rebate?
No.
Why was that?
As you've just acknowledged, it's already working.
Taxpayer dollars are very precious.
Every dollar that we spend has an opportunity cost.
Every dollar spent
is a dollar that would otherwise be spent in our schools and our hospitals on
public infrastructure. And so in a very tight fiscal environment, where
we have a scheme that's already working and already supporting growth, it hasn't
been a priority to expand it.
So part of the
strength of that scheme is that it stops companies leaving New Zealand.
Have you conducted any work or seen any research to
suggest that the settings as they are currently are sufficient to stop
some of our most successful companies leaving?
Well, as the member literally acknowledged in the framing for your question, we've
had 40% growth in the sector year on year, which would
suggest that it's a sector that's doing very well.
Yes. And are you aware of the
rebates and enticements being offered by other
countries for very successful companies in New Zealand to move
offshore and potentially have better support from
other governments?
Similar to the film subsidy, I'm aware that there will always be countries that,
for many reasons, can afford to be more generous.
Sometimes because they've had more fiscally disciplined governments in the past
that have put them in a stronger position than New Zealand finds itself in after
six years of a labor government. Sometimes because they are
making different trade-offs than we're making.
As we entered into this budget, and in fact each of the three budgets, we have
had on our hands a massive task of repairing the deficit left
to us by the last government as a structural impediment.
Overcoming the debt mountain, which had exploded and more than doubled while
Labor led the government. And we have been having to overcome those two
things while also making essentially essential investments
in critical public services in our schools and our hospitals and worn
down public infrastructure. And we have been prioritizing those things, and at the
same time have sustained what is a generous
rebate for the gaming sector. And I am really pleased to see the
success of that sector. I am very pleased to see it working, and I haven't
seen any evidence to suggest that there is a pressing
need to reform those settings when we are achieving so much growth within
that sector.
Sure. So in that answer, you're talking about responsible fiscal management of
government expenditure-
Mm
... keeping a tight lid on expenditure.
Are you happy that a minister in your government
overspent by more than $30,000 on a four-day trip overseas?
Madam Chair, I'm not sure that that's within the- Yeah. All right ...
jurisdiction of this committee. Do you have any more questions?
I've got more primary questions.
Okay. We move on to Pushta Ganghi Manu.
Thank you, Madam Chair. Minister, do you remain committed to doubling
the Maori economy by 2035? And if so, do
you have any targeted policies or will you introduce any targeted
policies to achieve that?
Well, one of the things that's really compelling about the Maori economy is
the significant growth that has been occurring within it.
And there's two parts to that. Partly that
growth has been tied to the fact that we have seen so much success
happening in our primary industries, and you do see that a number of Maori
businesses are either whenua related or are
related to exporting food, fiber, other products of the
land. Equally, we've seen diversification of the Maori economy.
We're increasing rates of Maori entrepreneurship in terms of small
businesses owned by Maori, in which there are Maori
workers have been flourishing. And so I think it's wrong to
kind of say, well, the Maori economy is always a primary industry based economy.
That's not what the data supports.
Equally, when we look at our goal of export double,
over the next decade, which we're by the way, on track with because
we've had significant export growth, in recent years.
I see Maori exporters as being a huge part of that,
both in terms of ownership of farms, but also value added
products. And many of those exporters are supported
directly by NZTE, who work with focus
businesses that they think have the scope to go global to provide them connections
into international markets. An example that comes to mind for me,
who I've visited in Whakatane is Waimanuka.
Some members will have tried the product. It's a fizzy honey drink.
It's really delicious, and by the way, it's good with whiskey.
And look, they've been working with NZTE to get
that product to market. And a big part of the provenance story, which they're
selling when you buy that product in New York or elsewhere, is that it's
got this unique Maori cultural identity behind it.
And I think that
for a modern day consumer who's prepared to spend a lot of money on a premium
product, that cultural identity story has some value.
So I've got confidence that the Maori economy will grow, but
I think a large part of that will be through export, not just domestic
businesses.
I actually used to work with Joe-
Ah
... who developed Waimaanuka, so-
Wonderful man.
Yeah. I can testify.
But that said, all the acknowledgments you've made, like I said with progressive
procurement, that gave a guaranteed chunk of the pie to ensure that
development. So will you introduce any...
You've mentioned exports.
Mm-hmm.
A portion, some export targets for Māori business.
Well, as I say, I'm seeing so much success across the Māori
economy that I am confident that those businesses, without a
quota, will still be getting support from government
agencies. I know
that NZTE is already working with many Māori
businesses. I know that the changes our government is making across the
board are very facilitative of Māori business.
One of the things that I think is interesting with the FastTrack Act is
that actually some of the applicants for having projects fast-tracked are
iwi or iwi-led organizations, and that's because we
have iwi with significant amounts of capital who want to get on and build
things and develop things on their land with their resources.
And often it's not so much that they're coming to the government saying, "Here's my
cat, can you put something in it?" They're actually saying, "Government, can you
get out of the way? You're putting red tape in the way.
You're stopping us from doing the development, the innovation, the
entrepreneurship that we would like to be doing." And so our government's approach
is to say, wherever we can, let's remove those barriers, and I'm very confident
that that approach supports all businesses, Māori and non-Māori.
Member Davidson.
Thank you.
Wanted to talk about artificial intelligence.
What was the department's AI spend for the last two years, and what's
budgeted going forward for AI in '26?
Again, I have a good couple of pages on this.
So
obviously,
many
staff at MBIE have access to
Copilot, and
I get feedback that they
are seeing as an increasingly important tool for their work.
AI is also deployed in some of the systems through which
they deliver services to New Zealanders, so it is a feature
of some of the immigration system in terms of the checks that need to be carried
out there. And also it is something where MBIE,
I believe, have done pilots with some of their call centers to
provide better logs of calls, better quality assurance.
I am... Does someone know which page it is in my folder? I'm flicking through.
36. There you go.
No minister can operate without a great team.
And so in terms of AI, the overall
figure, as I said,
the ministry has deployed several AI tools, Microsoft Copilot,
Copilot Chat, Copilot Researcher, Genesys Cloud Supervisor AI,
internal training being delivered to introduce staff to key AI concepts,
and as MBIE is developing a regular reporting on the AI program of work, including
investment plans for the future. And when they do those plans, that
includes the AI, the adoption, the risks and the issues.
Since early '25, almost half of MBIE staff have had access to
Copilot to chat,
and usage was showing strong and growing engagement with that.
They also have a capability program, which they are
building skills through MBIE. And in terms of delivery,
a key customer-facing example is the integration of AI
into the call center platform, as I talked about, Genesys Cloud, and that's about
automated call summarization, real-time note generation, voice-enabled IVR
and chatbots. And the benefits for the New Zealander on the other end of
that include reduced wait times, clearer insights into what they're actually trying
to achieve, and more streamlined processes.
AI is also being embedded into the Nexus ministerial services workflow
tool,
and policy teams are using AI to accelerate some of their
work.
Right. Can you tell me more about what that voice-enabled IVR is?
The call center you're talking about?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I can say a bit about that. It's similar to what other departments like
IR and ACC have been doing, so it's being able to
listen to the call effectively, do the transcript, and do the summary notes.
So those are the bits that can take quite a bit of time at the end of a
call for
case managers. And so being able to do that quickly has helped
save time, makes it more efficient. So that's the pilots.
We're more in the pilot phase of that at the moment.
Sure. And just further to that, because the question was around the cost-
Mm-hmm
... both incurred thus far and forecast into the future for those, and there was a
bit of detail in the answer, but there wasn't a dollar figure attached.
So I don't have the full data that you're asking for.
I do know that the ministry's purchase of 970 Microsoft
Copilot 365 licenses came in at a cost of
'25, '26 year. That's excluding GST.
In terms of overall AI spend, I'm very happy to come back to the
member in writing.
And do you suspect,
or do you know whether there's an increase in that spend or forecast
expenditure?
I don't know, but I would expect there to be.
Because the way that I conceptualize AI is if we think about
the past 20 years, what we have seen across New Zealand
businesses and public service agencies is increasing proportion
of investment happening in digital tools. Why?
To ensure better service to the public, and to assure more
efficient use of taxpayer resources.
As we look to the next decade AI is, in a sense, the
next wave of digital tools.
It's about how do we get better services for people, better
informed services, more responsive services, more tailored
services, but do that in a way that achieves maximum value for money.
And so where AI can be used to support a better outcome for the
New Zealander interacting with government, then it is something that we
expect government agencies to consider, but to do so very
mindfully. All of it needs to be used within an ethical framework with data
protections, privacy protections, and in a way that is always tested
against, okay, well, if the hoped-for benefit was that it would make it better for
the consumer on the other end, did it achieve that?
Sure. And that's good to hear, because that mindful approach is something we're
hearing more and more is required-
Absolutely
... especially from the countries that are ahead of us.
Yes.
Are you satisfied that you've got appropriate safeguards in place?
I am satisfied that government agencies take
those protections and controls very
seriously, both in terms of the governance of AI, the
data protocols, and the privacy protocols.
I also fully expect that we need to keep retesting
those.
There are principles and policies in place.
Making the Public Service Commissioner now the overall
head of digital for the public service, I think is important, because as
we look to the future, making sure that we have
ethical rules which keep up to
date with technology development, I think does require a center of
accountability. And I think it's appropriate that the leader of the public
service is the leader of that accountability, because this is a
technology that is changing extremely fast.
And so we can't stay still. We can't say, "Look, we've done it.
We've ticked that box. We've got all the protections and protocols in place,"
because I fully expect they will continue to need to evolve as the technology
evolves.
So just to final up on that,
I think.
The people who do those jobs, who work in that space with AI,
with tech, with-
Mm
... all the areas that you've talked about being growth areas, have traditionally
been known as back room or back office roles.
Mm.
You've been very
vocal and confident about the need to reduce back office roles.
Mm-hmm.
How does that marry up with what you're talking about today, about the
need for significant and serious investment in exactly the same
space?
Because what we're working to achieve is that we get more
impact for every dollar that we're investing.
And so if what I can have is one person in a call
center who is doing a beautiful job of
empathetically engaging with the people who call them, but
instead of then having to spend half their time taking call
logs, analyzing previous notes,
writing synthesis and analysis, and instead an AI tool does that for them,
then effectively I'm doubling their productivity.
So what that means is that we can make AI investments that
mean that people in administrative and support roles can
be more effective with the resources that they have.
Which should mean, as we look to the future, it's very realistic to
say that the New Zealand Public Service can be
proportionately more like it's been in the past, which is to say around
1% of the population. It exploded to
1.2% under the
last government, and we don't think it's unrealistic to say that over the next few
years we can get it back to 1%, given all of the modern tools
available to us.
Thank you for the example. It's a good one.
We all dream of days where we have wonderful, friendly, and available people
in call centers to- ... press when you dial the number.
Yeah.
But I would argue that a call center operator is a frontline role,
and in fact, the systems and services that they rely on,
be it AI or IT,
require a level of human engagement and
interaction to ensure that they are operating and that they are safe.
So in your example, the risk is that it doesn't
account for the human resource still required to
ensure those systems are operating with the kind of integrity
that I think all responsible governments would want to know is the case.
Well, you can take a glass half empty approach, as is sort of your role in
opposition, I guess. But my approach is to say,
actually, how do we ensure a better service to the public
with better value for money? And I think that the use of AI
gives us way more options about how we do that.
And often actually, it is welcomed by workers
themselves. The example I'd give you of that was the trial that Health
New Zealand ran of using AI scribes in emergency
rooms where,
for many years, nurses and health practitioners, doctors have said,
"Look, the time you have to spend in the emergency room taking notes, uploading
them, getting them into the right systems, is time that directly takes you
away from being with the people who are waiting in the queue in the
emergency room." And of course, that's what doctors and nurses want to be doing.

value for taxpayers in the investments that we're making.

and
organizations looked at savings because we needed to.
In terms of overall government spending, it was unsustainable, and in order to get

In that,

Tourism and conservation.

new supply initiatives.

35 million of new spending,

As I've said, and I'm sure I said it here last year,

of
the discomfort that can come from growth that isn't

quarters there was some concern, and we can't do
that. So currently,
visitor community satisfaction with visitors,
and we need to maintain that level of social
license.
I have also said in my new
performance expectations from Tourism New Zealand-

in what I have set as expectations to the Board of

It is not growth at any cost.

Then, IVL ministers, the Minister of Finance,

Take those

23 Great Rides and fill the

I think at the very first one that we launched in 2009,

Fantastic

No.

So,
in terms of the numbers,
we had--

return on investment,

Okay.
Right? So this view that I've sat around and dreamt up

at the end of it,

Australia's states don't disclose the values of those

and say, "This is the economic impact delivered from this

it will then determine what we do with the event

concerts
delivered
whatever amount per dollar investment, and that's totally worth

It might say that this other type of event-
Right ... was a bomb, and we shouldn't be doing that in future.
And,
yep, that's on me, and I'm happy
to be in a position where we have
invested in a much wider range of events across the
country than we would have otherwise.
And as I said, I think the numbers when they come in, World
Surf League, within a day of the event starting-
The organizers were like, "Wow, what an
amazing place for us to hold this event."

I'm looking to Renee for this, is you might
have, say, 15,000, 20,000 physical visitors

World Surfing League is in the top five
sports of international
social media reach.
Let's see your help too.
And so the value of that for New Zealand,
we'll attempt to measure it.
Mm.
But it is actually quite hard to do.
Do you want to-
Yeah
... do you want to add to that?
Yeah. Certainly when you look at events, those are the two key metrics,

Estimated advertising value, if I was to buy that space, it would cost me X

Mm.
Finals-
Yeah. I guess, and I hear what you're saying, and I get with the commercially

We're not paying the artist. So let me be clear, we're not paying the

What the recognition was, was for some
artists,
Linkin Park sold out that arena,
had the highest audience they've ever had in, it's not Spark Arena

Doesn't matter.
And so they wouldn't have come to New
Zealand without us providing some financial
support, which covers the costs
of them traveling to get here, right?
So the money does not go into the artists' pockets.
One of the things we're looking at, which again, has been feedback we've
received, is
can we provide greater opportunity through that
international artists to then have the
pre-performer
or the warm-up performer
as local.
What is also not well represented is
every component of that concert
supports New Zealand businesses.
The lighting, the riggers, the staging, the caterers,

the hotels that look after the artists,
you name it. That all goes into the pockets of New Zealand
businesses. And one of the early comments I heard from the events

is,
it's really hard for those businesses to survive if they're only doing

But if they've got some international conferences that they're doing, and they're

If they're doing a wide range, then it means that their businesses

to invest, take on staff, et cetera.
So it's
those sorts of benefits
that,
probably to be fair, that we didn't communicate well enough
early on.
All right. Minister, we'll have to conclude there. Thank you so much for your time.
Thank you.
And we will get some written questions later on.
Sure.
Thank you.
Thank you for your time. Hope the rest of your afternoon goes well.
